Transcrição Completa: 3 Josh Troy - Sales Talk Part 2     [00:00]  "Yeah. So, as it says, I'm going to talk about the one thing I never talk about, which is sales. Now, I actually meant that to be literal because uh a lot of the times, for those of you guys who have heard me speak in the past or maybe somewhere else, I'm always just talking about like scaling and operations."   [00:20]  "And last time was on financial modeling and budgets because I just like that stuff a lot. Um, I don't really ever almost uh I can't even remember the last time I actually talked about like sales because I don't go to these things to be like a sales trainer. I'm not speaking to reps like I'm speaking to business owners that want to scale, right?" Like how do you develop your pitch? Um, what does your talk track sound like? How do you create that? what a reps actually say on the calls. Um just your general approach about it, right? A lot of questions on this when I was asking what I should speak on here."   [01:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pitch Design Formula & Anatomy of Talk Track"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Dois componentes principais da apresentação   [01:18]  "So, I'm going to do this. I'm gonna try to go through this one faster and hopefully you get something out of it. All right. So, I'm going to talk about the pitch design formula and the anatomy of the talk track, which is like our script builder."   [01:35]  "was talking to a couple people on the break um or at the break and I was explaining the reason I don't call it a script is because typically scripts are very word for word and they don't really work right in sales you know if you're like in customer service and you outsource to India or whatever it is like maybe that works"   [01:58]  "but in sales what I like to do is I want to guide and give parameters to the flow of the conversation but allow critical thinking for them to be fluid in the conversation. And we do that with a talk track. So it has direction and they treat the questions and the things to say as like a toolbox, but you're not using every tool every time. Right? That's what I mean when I say talk track."   [02:28]  "Um, by the way, um, I always I think it's interesting in this space, too. Like I've I've been using talk track for a lot of years. I've never I never heard anyone say that a lot of years ago. And now I hear a lot of people talking about talk track."   [02:45]  "You guys ever think about like, damn, I wish there was a way to prove that I came up with something, but then like it goes from one person to another and years later it's just like a thing. Maybe I didn't invent it, but it's uh Anyways, I think that's kind of funny when you hear those things."   [03:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "What I Always Hear On Sales Calls"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Lista de problemas comuns em sales calls   [03:10]  "All right, so here's what I wanted to talk about. Like I'm going to start by talking about when I go into deals. Like I said, they there's always a sales team in place because um we're not doing like zero pre-revenue or zero to one types of motions, right?"   [03:28]  "So they're always doing revenue. Typically they're doing a couple hundred. Our largest deal they were doing over three million a month when they brought us in."   [03:40]  "And actually that's an interesting point because uh I thought a lot a long time ago years ago when I started this business that the only people that would outsource to us is because of the lack of capability."   [03:55]  "But some of our best clients actually know sales very well. And they know it so well that they know how much there is to it. Like the last presentation I just did. and they don't have an ego and they're smart and they want to scale fast and they say, 'Hey, if I just bring in an expert that knows this stuff, I could focus on these other areas.'"   [04:20]  "So, surprisingly, if you didn't know that, uh we actually yeah, we get brought into deals that do significant revenue already and then they just hand it over to us."   [04:32]  "And so, what happens because of that, like I said, everything you want to know is on the calls. So, um, I listen to a lot of calls, not that many from my own team anymore because our sales team does that or our sales management team does that like in the last presentation."   [04:52]  "But I'm trying to understand what I'm walking into, especially if the handoff is because of low performance. That's critical for me like because the second I say yes, their problems become mine, right? And so I want to know like, well, what exactly is the issue? And I go through the validation sequence and all of those things." [06:28]  "Um people that just say got it in between every question like it's an interrogation. You know, they ask the question, got it, got it. And let me ask you this. Boom. Got it. Got it. And let me ask you this. And it's like so hard to actually listen to that call."   [06:48]  "That's another thing that's interesting about having your sales reps listen to calls. Like some sometimes don't even do a call review. If you hear a bad call, just be like, 'Dude, go listen to that call. Tell me what you think.' Cuz they don't realize how bad it sounds when they're in the moment."   [07:08]  "So sometimes it's really powerful to just have them listen to a bad call and tell you what they did wrong. Right? But anyways, uh and they're just feature dumping rather than delivering an intentional pitch."   [07:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "The Big Three"   NÚMEROS:  3 componentes essenciais   VISUAL:  Strong Discovery, Irresistible Personalized Pitch, Closing Sequence   [07:30]  "So, remember I was talking about the big three. A strong discovery, an irresistible personalized pitch, and a closing sequence. I mean, if you don't learn anything else in sales, learn how to do that and you'll do better than most."   [07:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Main Reasons People Lose Deals"   NÚMEROS:  3 razões principais   VISUAL:  1. Weak Discovery, 2. Weak Pitch, 3. Can't Close   [07:52]  "But these are the main reasons that people lose deals. I'm going to start with this and we'll segue into how we do it. So, weak [clears throat] discovery. They're not asking good questions. They're checking boxes. It's an interrogation. They don't demonstrate value or expertise. they don't expand the gap and amplify desire."   [08:18]  "The second thing is a weak pitch. Usually, by the way, as a lack or as a result of a weak discovery, they're creating objections, pitching features in a non-compelling way. It doesn't align with the gap or core desires they learn, and there's a value perception issue."   [08:40]  "Number three is they can't close. Um, they don't ask for the business, like I was saying earlier, which is always surprising. It's so surprising. I don't I'll never understand that one. I start with the offer in the end and the end in mind and I reverse engineer to design a very strategic talk track."   [10:22]  "Like for some reason it always comes to mind is like a comedian when they're setting setting up for a punchline. [10:40]  "It's the same here is like you have to build everything strategically knowing where you're taking them. So, I always start with the pitch and I reverse engineer."   [10:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pitch Design Philosophy"   NÚMEROS:  Aumento significativo em close rates (número não especificado por compliance)   VISUAL:  Diferença entre Offer (o que você dá) vs Pitch (como você vende)   [10:58]  "Now, the delivery of the pitch makes all the difference. And I don't have to worry about FTC stuff when I'm on this stage because I'm not selling an offer. I'm just generally telling you what I do and how to do it. But I will tell you without knowing the specific number, you can increase your close rates significantly just by building a pitch."   [11:22]  "Because the reality of it is, especially in this industry, people don't pitch. They like read what the product is, you know? And and this will become a lot more clear here in a second when I go through the slides."   [11:38]  "Offer is what you give them, right? Offer is what they get. Pitch is how you sell it. Right? So when I talk about the concept of pitch design, how to design your pitch, it's how you communicate your offer in a very clear, concise, and compelling way with a unique delivery that's unlike anything they've ever seen before."   [12:05]  "And you break it down in a way where the core value propositions of the offer connect directly to their pain points and core desires. Directly to their pain points and core desires. Right?" The the most jarring slashconfusing thing is almost nobody ever has a pitch."   [12:48]  "I see some good, not everything's horrible. I see some good things on sales calls. The thing I see most commonly is they don't have a pitch at all. There's no pitch. You guys might even be looking at me right now like, 'What exactly does he mean by pitch?' you know, is it just like explaining the offer, right?"   [13:12]  "Uh all they do is a really uh they do weak screen shares. It's a screen share close and they explain the offer in a way that could be true about any other offer."   [13:28]  "Here's what I mean when I say it could be true about any other offer. [13:48]  "And then they explain the same fuck every call on every offer. If you're an education brand, you know this. They say, 'Yeah, so basically the first thing that you have is all the on demand curriculum.' And then they'll explain all the things that are included in the on demand curriculum."   [14:10]  "And then with the on demand curriculum, every week we have coaching calls. And they'll explain the whole live coaching call component. And then they'll say, 'And then on top of that, we have our resource vault.' So we have all the resources and the documents and blah blah blah blah blah."   [14:30]  "And it's the same thing for every offer. Like you could close your eyes and it could quite literally be anything, right? That's not a pitch. You're just like explaining. I don't even know how to explain it. It's like you're explaining subject like subjective features that are generally facts about the thing you're selling, but it has nothing to do with sales." This is what makes us stand out, right? Pitch design can literally be the difference of millions of dollars, right? I guess it depends what level of revenue you're at, but it makes a big difference, right? It makes a really big difference." They proceed to describe the features and inclusions of the offer in a really generic way. It's lifeless, boring, and generic, which will get you lifeless, boring, generic results."   [16:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Marketing vs Sales Disconnect"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  USPs, Positioning, Key Differentiators devem ser consistentes   [16:20]  "All right. So, one thing that I think is really ironic is when I'm talking about pitch design in sales, it's like a foreign concept. Like, like I just said, almost no one ever does it. But it's so weird to me because people in marketing, they spend so much time on this stuff."   [16:40]  "You guys spend so much time on uh USPs, unique selling propositions, positioning, key differentiators, right? Like that's every you guys spend so much time on that in your marketing messaging and then it's almost like the sales guys are like, 'Well, fuck it. That fuck doesn't matter.' It's like it's the same thing. It's the same exact thing."   [17:05]  "And and by the way, it should be perfectly congruent. the USPS and the differentiators that got their attention to begin with should be pulled through into the sales call. Right?"   [17:20]  "There's actually there's so many other things I could go into on this which I won't right now, but it creates a confirmation bias. There's a reinforcement bias. There's it's it's congruent. There's a lot of marketers that call it a through line, right?"   [17:38]  "Like if you have an event and you have your marketing and your ads, the content should match the marketing and the ads. The sales call should match the content. Like you want it to be consistent all the way through. So there's not this disconnect."   [17:55]  "So most of the time, if you have all that stuff done for marketing, you could use the same stuff to help develop your sales pitch."   [18:08]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Generic Pitch vs Irresistible Pitch - Steak Example"   NÚMEROS:  12 ounce, 14 days aging   VISUAL:  Comparação lado a lado de pitch genérico vs irresistível   [18:12]  "All right. Now, I'm going to give you an example. I'm not going to do it to specific to an offer. I'm going to do it to something that none of us actually sell, but I just want to make it extremely clear. Here's a generic pitch, and here's what I hear on calls all the time."   [18:32]  "Okay, you guys obviously will know what this is that I'm selling. So, our stake is a 12. And I'm not even going to exaggerate it. I'll try to do it good like they would do it. Yeah. So, our steak is a 12 ounce cut. It's cooked to your liking and then it's served with mashed potatoes and vegetables. You're also able to choose from sauces like peppercorn and mushroom sauce."   [19:00]  "Okay, that's a generic pitch. Here's an irresistible pitch."   [19:05]  "So, our signature steak, man, it really sets you up for a luxurious dining experience. I mean, if you're a foodie looking for a meal to yelp yelp about, the preparation is perfectly seared, hand selected, prime cut that's aged for 14 days with our proprietary seasoning blend"   [19:28]  "and then it's lightly charred for a sharp cris for a sharp crisp and finished off over a grill in an open flame. We're known for a process called our artisian flame technique. So the char, the way that it works, the char locks in the rich, savory flavors. You literally won't find this anywhere else."   [19:52]  "And then as soon as the knife like penetrates the crust, it falls to the bottom of the plate, cutting like butter. We then pair that with a unique blend of mushrooms. I don't even know how the chef does it. It's really unique. And it creates this like explosion of flavor together."   [20:15]  "So I mean listen you could get a steak almost anywhere but you would be hardressed to find another location frankly in the entire country that has perfected this preparation style like we have. Right?"   [20:32]  "Listen the reason I choose steak as an example is because I want it to be exaggerated. Right? I want to understand like which one sounds better. Right? Obviously there's nobody here that's telling the truth that would say the first one."   [20:48]  "This is the difference when you're developing a pitch. Now, notice I wasn't just overly descriptive. I was positioning it differently. I was I was uh I was differentiating it. I was making it clear you couldn't get anywhere else. I was almost creating some psychological FOMO, right?"   [21:10]  "You're like, 'Dude, fuck.' Like, it's so much more vivid in their mind of why they need it and why they should buy it. Okay, so that's what we want to accomplish with our pitch."   [21:25]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Components of Great Pitch: Story - Pitch - Pillars"   NÚMEROS:  3 componentes   VISUAL:  Estrutura sequencial do pitch perfeito   [21:30]  "Now, what are the components or a structure of a great pitch as we segue into it? The way that we deliver our pitch is story pitch pillars. Very, very simple. Story pitch pillars, right?"   [21:48]  "This is, by the way, the most fluid way to get into it. Like when we trans transition into a pitch, I always like to say, 'Yeah, let me just give you like a quick background of like why we even created this and why we do what we do.' You give the story, give the pitch, and then you break down the main pillars of the offer."   [22:12]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pitch Design Questionnaire"   NÚMEROS:  3 sets de perguntas   VISUAL:  1. Subject Matter Expert/Credibility, 2. Domain Expertise, 3. Offer/Proprietary Method   [22:18]  "And so what we do is we we have a pitch design questionnaire. And there's three different sets of questions we go through. We have subject matter expert, credibility, and background questions of like what's specific to this person and subject matter expert."   [22:38]  "Then we go over domain expertise and subject matter focus, right? So like all the things that are true about that general industry. And then the third thing is the offer and the proprietary method like the USPS and the key differentiators."   [22:58]  "Like why is the thing that you do different than anyone else that does the same thing that you do, right? In all of honesty, guys, like please be honest here. Raise your hand if your sales reps are really doing that on the calls. One guy kind of."   [23:18]  "All right. Thanks for your honesty. Yeah, I mean this this is like such a big difference because it's the same way you're going to stand stand out in marketing. This is what's going to win the deals on the phones."   [23:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Story = Mirror Existence Narrative"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Definição de narrative que reflete a história do prospect   [23:40]  "All right, so let me break down each element. So um the first one is a is a story, right? I told you story pitch pillars. Now when we say story, I call it a mere existence narrative. Right?"   [23:58]  "Now I'll just read it word for word. This is a compelling background story that's more than a collection of accolades or achievement. It's an opportunity to deeply connect with your audience."   [24:15]  "The existence narrative explains the how and the why behind your offer in a way that makes makes your audience feel like it was specifically designed for them. The goal is for them to see their own story reflected in yours. Hence, mirror narrative, fostering a sense of alignment and shared purpose. Right?"   [24:40]  "So, we start with the story. So, I would say something like, uh, all right, Devin, give me a quick example. Well, if you were to outsource sales to me, what's the reason you would outsource?"   [24:55]  "Um, I would say a lack of capacity. It's taking up all of my time and I can't focus on"   [25:02]  "All right, perfect. Let's stop there. He said, 'A lack of capacity. Can't focus on ever other things.' So, if I'm going into a story, say, 'Awesome, dude. Well, I really appreciate you sharing that. First of all, I'd do an information confirmation, which I'll show you guys how to do that later.'"   [25:22]  "But then I would say, 'Yeah.' So, let me go ahead and break this down. First of all, the reason I started this business is because, you know, I was working with a lot of highlevel individuals and the main thing that I noticed is that they get so busy scaling the thing that they're they're scaling"   [25:42]  "that the thing that they love most ends up being the thing that they hate most because instead of owning their business, the business owns them. And so what happens is they start hitting this constraint because even though they have the skill sets themselves, they don't have the capacity to continue to scale beyond that."   [26:05]  "So what I find and the reason we built this, we're not trying to look for people that don't understand how to scale a business. We want people that know how to scale a business but prefer to spend their time in other places and then bring us in as a sales sales experts to bring way more capacity to help them scale. Right?"   [26:28]  "That's like the background narrative as a madeup example here is like the point is when I tell my story, he hears his. It's like perfectly congruent to what he's doing. Right?"   [26:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Sizzle Pitch = 30-60 Second Elevator Pitch"   NÚMEROS:  30-60 segundos   VISUAL:  Pitch diferenciado e sexy em formato curto   [26:48]  "The sizzle pitch is I'm not even going to read it. It's like an elevator pitch, right? It's like a 30 se 30 to 60 second pitch that highly differentiates what you do in a way that just sounds really sexy."   [27:05]  "And you guys just there's so many different examples of this. Like I had there was an offer, you know, uh that we worked with and it was building a rental property. This is the first one that came to mind for some reason."   [27:22]  "And the way that their sales reps would explain it is they'd be like, 'Awesome.' They'd share their screen. So the way that this works is we have our on demand curriculum and they went into the whole fuck I just told you."   [27:38]  "So what we changed it to is they'd say awesome. Well, let me tell you a little bit about John. John's really unique in a sense because there's a lot of people out there that teach real estate education and show you how to build a rental property."   [27:55]  "I don't know if you've noticed, but we actually target a lot more affluent audiences because what John noticed is that there's a lot of real estate education companies out there that aren't focused on strategies that make sense for already successful people."   [28:15]  "They're showing you how to like cold call and pull lists and do offmarket deals. But the people that we work with don't want to do any of that fuck They're looking for a strategy that they could leverage that's way more efficient that they could do with their full-time job and build a portfolio of passive income in the background."   [28:38]  "And so he developed what he calls his endless equity strategy. That's exactly how it sounds. It leverages equity in existing property in a really effortless way to continue to acquire more that self-sustains the debt service. You see the difference?"   [28:58]  "It's so different. It's so different because what I just said is exactly what they're looking for and it's different. The first thing is any real estate education offer ever."   [29:15]  "So again like this is you know somebody asked me a question earlier and I really appreciated the question and he said you know well how do you create that urgency if you know our people whatever all I heard was urgency that's what I focused on"   [29:35]  "this is the type of stuff that builds urgency because people are like well why why is my sales cycle so long and why can't I build urgency because you just pitched the same fuck that they seen six other times and now they're stuck trying to make a decision on which offer to buy and they have no evidence."   [29:58]  "They have no information on like how to make that decision. So, people don't act. They just say, 'Oh, I'll get back to you.' Right? They need that conviction that it's different than anything else."   [30:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pillars = Foundational Components"   NÚMEROS:  3-4 pilares principais   VISUAL:  Estrutura simplificada do offer em componentes principais   [30:20]  "Pillars are the foundational components of the offer. A confused mind doesn't buy. A confused mind doesn't buy. You don't want to go into just like throwing up and we call it feature dumping. You're like, 'Ann it has this and this and this and this and this.'"   [30:42]  "come up with like three to four main thing that encapsulate what you do and just focus on pitching those things. The reason why is because they need clarity too. They need clarity to buy."   [31:00]  "If you just give them a story and a pitch every I promise you with like 90% of the time they'll be like, 'Dude, that sounds awesome, but like what is it?' You know, that's what they'll say."   [31:15]  "So, you still have to give them the pillars, but you simplify it into like, 'All right, so the first thing that you get is this. This is the first thing.' And you like, you know, you outline the main pillars. It's story pitch pillars. That's how we pitch. So you should literally memorize that word for word."   [31:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pitch Personalization = Step 7 of 10"   NÚMEROS:  Step 7 de 10 steps   VISUAL:  Conectar features aos core desires e pain points   [31:45]  "The only thing that changes is pitch personalization. Pitch pitch personalization for us in the 10 steps to wires from st strangers is step number seven. And it's exactly how it sounds. We personalize the pitch to their dominant buying motives to their core desires to their pain points. Right?"   [32:10]  "And so it's just the very simple the very simple process of connecting the feature back to the benefit and the core desire. So you'd say something like, you know, yeah. Yeah. So, the first thing that we do is uh we show you how to get, you know, um"   [32:30]  "so the first thing that we do is we show you I don't know the the name of the or I don't know what that actual offer has anymore, so I'm going to make this up. The first thing that we do is called on market mastery. And so, like you can't go anywhere without deal flow."   [32:50]  "Now, what we do remember earlier when you were telling me that the number one concern you have and why you haven't built a portfolio earlier than this is because you have a job and one of the hardest things is these other offers are showing you offmarket fuck and you have a full-time job. Yeah."   [33:15]  "Because of that, that's exactly why we built the onmarket mastery program. And so the way that it works and then I explain it. So pitch personalization is just the concept of connecting it back because you'd be surprised in sales how many prospects aren't able to connect the dots."   [33:38]  "And so what happens is there's a massive disconnect. You do this massive discovery. You find the pain points. You find out what they care about and then you do a pitch over here and they're not connecting the dots."   [33:55]  "So you literally have to treat them like a baby and be like, 'Dude, remember earlier you're saying about this?' Yeah, that's exactly why we built that. And then you ask him, 'Wouldn't that make a big difference?'"   [34:12]  "Hey, by the way, how does your confidence level change knowing that you would be able to actually build deal flow without it affecting your day job at all? Would that make a big difference? Yeah, it would. So, just to confirm, your confidence level that my program will work for you is way higher than the ones that haven't worked in the past. Yeah, it would. Okay, awesome. And you go on, right? You're just tying them down."   [34:42]  "So, that's p personalization."   [34:48]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Talk Track Development - Two Part Close Process"   NÚMEROS:  2 partes   VISUAL:  Part 1: Intro & Discovery, Part 2: Pitch & Close   [34:52]  "So, once you have your pitch, obviously this could be a several hourong thing, which it won't be. So, I'm just going to keep on going. Once you have your pitch, we have talk track development."   [35:08]  "So, it's a two-part close process. I didn't say two call, I said two parts. There's in uh intro and discovery. There's pitching and closing. That's really it in a sales process. You have your intro and your discovery. You transition to your pitch and your close."   [35:30]  "That could be on one call, that could be on two calls, that could be on several calls."   [35:38]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Should It Be One or Two Call Close?"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Pergunta comum sobre estrutura de calls   [35:42]  "The question always becomes, should it be a one or two call close? Before you read my slides, raise your hand if that's a question of yours. I see like six, but I know there's more because a lot of people have asked me this in the inner circle. This is a really common question I get. Should we have a one call close model or two or a two call close model?"   [36:08]  "I want to address this real quick before I go into the talk track development because it does slightly change the way you'd build your talk track."   [36:20]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "One vs Two Call Close Factors"   NÚMEROS:  Múltiplos fatores de decisão   VISUAL:  Level of Awareness, CPR (Clear Path to Revenue), Show Rate   [36:25]  "Here are the things that comes to mind. Number one is it depends on the level of awareness, right? If a lot of your calls are getting on with, yeah, wait, what is this about again? Wait, who's who's the guy? Like, I I remember seeing an ad like, what do you guys do? Dude, that's probably not a one call close scenario, right? Level of awareness matters a lot."   [36:52]  "Number two is CPR. Clear path to revenue, clear path to results. Jeremy and I talk about this a lot. I'll actually give you a quick example that's like not related to the one or two call close model, but it's very relevant as it comes to CPR."   [37:15]  "One of the number one things I evaluate in an irresistible offer is CPR. How clear is the path to the results they want? How clear is the path to the ROI or the revenue."   [37:32]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "CPR Example - Industrial Development"   NÚMEROS:  3 anos para ROI, $500K liquid, $50 cost per call, $400-500K/mês revenue   VISUAL:  Case study de offer com CPR ruim   [37:38]  "And uh I know there's a couple real estate development offers in here, so like you'll want to listen to this and you'll have to like figure out how to position this in your pitch or in your offer, whatever it is. We sold a uh industrial development offer. It was uh like flex space type buildings. if you guys have ever heard of those. It's a mixture of industrial and warehouse stuff."   [38:05]  "We did pretty well with it. Um, not great. We did, I think, 400 to 500 a month or so. We ended up walking away from the client because they wouldn't uh I respect those people, by the way. I'm not being rude, but they just wouldn't do the things that we needed them to do to change it because of the offer, which is this."   [38:30]  "The strategy that they taught took about three years to make money if they did it correctly." "Oh my god."   [38:40]  "three years. So, like we were getting the most qualified leads. Like I was actually really impressed. It was incredible. We were getting like qualified leads that all had like half a million dollars liquid for like 50 bucks a call. It was crazy."   [39:00]  "And I was very frustrated because I'm like, 'Yeah, why aren't we closing more of these? It didn't make any sense.' And we started really drilling into the calls. We went through the validation sequence. The number one thing was these people are like, 'Yeah, fuck that. I don't want to wait 3 years. I'm going to do this other thing.' Right?"   [39:22]  "or like a number one alternative to that offer was uh they just wanted to invest in a fund. So my point is that was not a clear path to results. The amount of uncertainty it created that there's three years in between the thing."   [39:42]  "I'm not saying lie that's never the answer but like is there a way you could get them a quicker win in the overall scheme? So, one of the things that we did, by the way, is we uh we broke it up into different phases and we and we what we ended up teaching, we said, 'Hey, yes, it takes 3 years for the full development"   [40:05]  "but we changed the offer and the pitch to um you could actually get uh I don't know the language of the offer. We sell a lot of stuff, but it was basically like get the raw land with development potential and then exit that to like the contractor to get some of your first deals, right?'"   [40:28]  "Focusing on that one thing alone made a massive difference on calls because the way that people psychologically connect the dots. It was so clear to them. They're like, 'Wait a second. So just in a matter of months, I could do XYZ instead of thinking 3 years and then imagine the objections that get created. What's going to happen with the economy? Do I even still want to do this in 3 years?'"   [40:55]  "So anyways, CPR is really important."   [41:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Show Rate Impact on Call Structure"   NÚMEROS:  60 minutos (one call) vs 30 minutos (two calls)   VISUAL:  Eficiência de atividade baseada em show rate   [41:05]  "Show up rate. Why? I said why there because everyone's always like, 'Well, why does show up rate have anything to do with it?' Here's why. If you have a one call close, you usually need an hour. If you have a two-part close, they're usually 30 minute calls. Okay?"   [41:28]  "You need more time if you're doing the full process on one call. If you have an atrocious show rate, the amount of downtime your reps have is significant. So, if you put it into what's called an activity model where you're basically measuring the the efficiency of or the economics, actually, it's not economics. That'd be a fin model."   [41:52]  "You're literally looking at the efficiency of the outputs. it doesn't make any sense. We would rather them have 15minute triage calls and only put the qualified ones in the next part of the process."   [42:10]  "But if you have a great level of awareness, a a really high CPR where they don't need a ton of information to make a decision, there's not a lot of complexity to the offer and you have really high show rates, one call close."   [42:30]  "It doesn't mean, by the way, every call closes in one call. It means you complete the process on one call. intro and discovery, pitching and closing. There might still be a follow-up for a decision call."   [42:48]  "All right, that's how you make that decision."   [42:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Anatomy of Talk Track"   NÚMEROS:  Múltiplos componentes   VISUAL:  Warm-up, Agenda Frame, Discovery, Information Confirmation, Transition, Pitch, Close   [42:58]  "So, here's the anatomy of the talk track. If you guys could read behind me, you'll see the note that says this has been modified for presentation purposes because it is deeper, but like we only have so much time here, guys, and it gets really really deep, but these are like the main elements of it."   [43:22]  "So when we build our talk track, we have a warm-up, we have an agenda frame, we have our discovery questions, we have our information confirmation, which is a strategic transition, and then you transition to the follow-up, you transition to the close. Then we have the pitch, which is story, pitch, pillars, and then our closing sequence."   [43:48]  "So let's go ahead and break down how we do all of them. Right?"   [43:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Build Process: Start With End In Mind"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Reverse engineering do pitch para discovery   [44:00]  "Uh we don't really need to talk about this slide. All it says is we start with the end in mind. We build the pitch first. We brainstorm the USPs and then we customize the questions and then we add the story pitch pillars."   [44:18]  "Like in the talk track other than developing the pitch, the only thing you're really doing is customizing the discovery questions. We have a set list of disco discovery questions, but we call them root questions because then you customize them to be specific and relevant to that brand and offer in avatar."   [44:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Agenda Frame Example"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Template completo de agenda frame   [44:48]  "All right, so I'll read this one word for word for the agenda frame. I think there's an example here. Um, oh, the whole thing's an example. All right, so here's an example of an agenda frame."   [45:05]  "Well, awesome, John. Yeah, I appreciate you applying to work with us. Now, I'm assuming you probably already know this by way of you scheduling a call, but we help people looking to insert common desired state and goals by insert activity."   [45:28]  "We teach in a compelling way, which can just be a really attractive option for a lot of people because of insert short statements summarizing why the model's so attractive to create initial awareness, which we're going to talk about a little bit more in a bit."   [45:48]  "Now, just to give you a quick overview for how we'll spend our time on these calls, they're super casual, really straightforward, and really they're just designed for me to first determine if we can actually help you based on your situation and expectations."   [46:10]  "After that, if we think that we could potentially be a good fit, of course, obviously, I'm going to give you a full breakdown, a full overview of how our program works. And from there, if you feel like this is checking all of your boxes, we can simply chat about what the next steps could look like."   [46:32]  "Or inversely, if not, no worries. Either way, my job is just to give you all the information that you would need to make an informed choice. Does that sound good? Awesome. Let's do it. I'm going to start by asking you some basic questions."   [46:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Pack Break Origin - Timeshare Industry"   NÚMEROS:  $50K purchase típico   VISUAL:  História da origem do pack break em timeshare   [46:58]  "All right. Now, why do I do that? Well, I'm going to tell you actually the development of this. So, this actually comes from time share back in the day. Now, timeshare, they are the most prolific salespeople typically historically because you are literally bribing people that say, 'I'm not buying fuck but I'll take the free whatever.' And then they go and then they spend 50K."   [47:28]  "So yeah, my business partner, our chief sales officer who manages all the sales directors, he was an executive for Windham timeshare and we have quite a few reps that we've taken from the uh time share industry."   [47:48]  "Now over the years, strategies do change and they evolve and the customer becomes more sophisticated. So back then it was called a hard pack break. You're breaking a pact and here's why."   [48:05]  "It actually developed in the time share space because what happens is, you know, you're bribed to be there. A husband and a wife would come in and they'd be like, 'All right, listen. We're going to go to this thing, right?' 'Yeah, we're going to go to this thing.'"   [48:25]  "All right. We're committing right now. No matter what they say, no matter how good it sounds, we're not buying anything. Yeah. No, no, I know, babe. We're just getting the freaking the hotel. All right. You sure? All right. We're not buying anything. Then they'd go in there and buy something."   [48:48]  "So what they did, the pack break was created because in the beginning of the sales pitch, it was a lot more aggressive back then. It would create sales resistance if you did it now, which is why it's modified to this."   [49:05]  "But the way that it was developed and the way that it worked is they'd say the the sales rep when they sit down, they'd say, 'All right, listen. So I'm going to show you what this is all about. I know you probably don't even want to see this presentation. You're just here because you're bribed, right?' They joke around with it. Yeah. Yeah. No, I get it."   [49:28]  "Listen, at the end of the day, like that's fine with me. Like I'm just here to show you the information and show you why our time shares are so attractive. At the end of this presentation, can I just ask you for one favor? Just give me a simple yes or a simple no."   [49:48]  "Don't tell me you have to call your aunt Marge to get advice. Don't tell me that your dog needs new braces next week, so you don't know if you can afford it yet. Just tell me a simple yes or a simple no. Is that fair? I can handle it. I'm a big boy. All right, perfect. and then they'd go into it."   [50:12]  "So, it's a one, not a call, but it's a one-sitting close in the time share space. So, literally, they go through that entire pitch. What happens through there, they build massive value. The way that time shares work is like it's it just gets people to be like, 'Holy fuck like I expect this to be way more money and all this stuff.'"   [50:38]  "And then at the end of it, they go to enroll them. And what happens is as soon as people are like, 'Well, can we talk about it?' Now, you're anchoring back to that and it helps you to isolate the objection. Right? long story, but to explain why that works."   [50:58]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Modern Pack Break - Removes Sales Resistance"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Versão moderna adaptada para remover resistência   [51:05]  "Now, that creates too much sales resistance these days. And there's a lot of trainers out there that will be uh I've even recently saw a real by one of them and she was like, 'Yeah, don't set the expectations at the beginning of the call because now they know that you're a sales rep.'"   [51:25]  "And I'm like, 'Dude, they all know we're sales reps.' Like, and I don't want to pretend I'm not. Like, I want the expectation that I'm here to sell something, but you do it with integrity and ethics and you remove sales resistance."   [51:42]  "So to me, just like anything in life, if you do it poorly, it won't work. But if you do it well, it works well. And so with the agenda frame, I'm setting the expectations so I can control the process."   [52:00]  "And guys, I'll tell you this. Here's what's really cool that I'm really proud to say. This is not like all the information that lives in my head because Josh is so smart and Josh is such an amazing salesperson. Our company's evolved so much beyond that."   [52:22]  "Everything I have is a byproduct of a shitload of the best salespeople in the world over thousands and thousands of sales calls, right? So, that's the cool thing. It's not just me. We we we're analyzing so many top performers and we're able to say why what they do works, why what others don't."   [52:48]  "And one of the things that I see when you skip an agenda frame, the rep uh the prospect starts asking a lot of questions. They try to drive the conversation, right? They want to know about the pitch. you didn't get any of the buy in. You didn't get permission. You didn't set the expectations."   [53:10]  "And then it builds sales resistance inversely because they don't know when you're going to sell them something. So, if you do it correctly, you got them right in the pocket. And as soon as they're like try to detour, you're like, 'Oh, hey, yeah, no, I totally get it. Hey, remember when I was telling you like, I want to ask all these things to make sure we could help in a little bit. I'm going to I'm going to anchor back to that.' Remember? And then they're like, 'Oh, yeah, right.'"   [53:38]  "So, it works really well. And then you'll notice when I read the template here in this example, um, it really it starts with opportunity awareness. So, we hook their interest right away. Like, yeah, by the way, like we know that most people that book these calls, it's cuz they're looking for a more attractive way to do XYZ."   [54:02]  "It's a great option if that's you and you're looking for blah blah blah. I'm going to talk about all of that later. So, we hooked them. They're like, 'Oh fuck I want to hear about that.' But we hooked them to the end of the call."   [54:18]  "All right. Good so far."   [54:22]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Types of Questions in Discovery"   NÚMEROS:  Múltiplos tipos   VISUAL:  Probing, Situational, Gap Awareness, Solution Awareness, Consequence/Impact, Commitment   [54:28]  "All right. Types of questions. Now, here's what I'm going to do on this. Questions are basic. They're pretty common. Uh, I buy a lot of stuff. I go through a lot of things because I want to know what's out there. I don't want to be right. I want to be rich."   [54:48]  "I've gone through like pretty much every sales training out there to see if they know something I don't. And it's usually a lot of the same stuff just repurposed in their own way. And so, these questions are pretty universal. I think the way we do them is pretty unique"   [55:10]  "and what I wanted to say these are the types of questions in a discovery uh uh process. What I wanted to show you is this which I haven't seen anyone talk about and I think this is the most significant part about the question types which is like what is the actual significance of each"   [55:32]  "cuz dude people don't understand this on sales calls you could tell by the way the rep asks they're not understanding the significance of the questions."   [55:45]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Question Types & Their Significance"   NÚMEROS:  6 tipos de perguntas   VISUAL:  Tabela com tipo de pergunta e sua significância específica   [55:50]  "We start with probing questions. This is just to direct our conversation. Hey, so let me ask you this, man. Like I'm sure you saw a lot of ads. Like, what specifically did you see in our ad that made you want to book a call with us today? They might tell you the number one thing that's going to steer all your attention the rest of the call. So, we start with some probing questions. That's the direct conversation."   [56:20]  "Next is situational questions. The significance of that is we're finding their pain points, their past attempts or things that didn't work, and their core desires. Right? It's current state versus desired state. That's what that tells us. Pain points and core desires."   [56:45]  "Gap awareness is where we expand the gap that we're twisting the knife. We know their pain points right here. We're twisting the knife. We're making them realize that that problem is much bigger, right? Um anyways, that's too long of an explanation. That's what that one is."   [57:08]  "The next is solution awareness. Solution awareness is where we amplify desire. Okay, pay attention to these next couple slides because I'm going to anchor back to this example in just a second. This is super important here."   [57:28]  "Solution awareness questions are where we're amplifying desire for our pitch. When you don't do that, it's just such a shitty discovery, for lack of a better way to describe it."   [57:45]  "Consequence and impact questions are where you create urgency and certainty. Right now, this one's really interesting because uh I saw recently, a little while back, not too not too long ago, where a lot of our reps uh on a specific offer, I should clarify, they were completely skipping the consequence questions and the commitment questions"   [58:10]  "for no reason other than the fact that the talk track was kind of long, so they'd run out of time and skip those since those are on the bottom. And like it's actually crazy like when you know exactly what you're looking for and you've listened to thousands of calls within seconds I could see what's happening"   [58:32]  "and I'm I'm looking at this I'm like okay yeah they're not asking consequence questions because what happens is it's such a lukewarm like you could see the difference the conversation seems so surface level because you get a lot of like compliance where they're like following the like yeah I could see that yeah that could work for me oh this is what I'm doing"   [58:58]  "but you never ask them Like, dude, let me ask you this. Like, if you didn't do this thing, how would you solve the problem? Oh, wow. Oh, so you literally have no other ideas. How do how does that make you feel? Like, if you're in the same situation 2 3 4 years from now, is that a is that that big of a deal?"   [59:22]  "Like, now you get into like the actual conversation and where you're like, 'Holy shit.' Okay. So, like this is like a now thing. Like, you have to solve this. Yeah, I have to solve this. And so like we were talking about earlier, without doing these things in discovery, you lose all of your power post pitch."   [59:48]  "Most things we do in discovery is to use before the pitch and after the pitch, right? Which is another thing that's crazy is a lot of reps. Well, I'll get to that in a second."   [1:00:02]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Commitment Questions = Objection Blocking"   NÚMEROS:  3 objeções principais   VISUAL:  Spousal/Partner, Money, Time - bloqueio pré-pitch   [1:00:08]  "All right. Then commitment questions is when we objection block, gauge the readiness and gauge the readiness to pitch. I was talking to a couple other sales guys um here uh a couple weeks ago. Like I said, I I love to learn. I'm always comparing notes, looking at other talk tracks."   [1:00:30]  "And uh this is something that they had that's very similar to ours, and I was happy to see it where in the commitment questions is where we're objection blocking. Guys, I'm going to tell you right now, here's the main objections you're going to get to every info product pitch."   [1:00:50]  "You're going to get a spousal spousal objection or a business partner objection. You're going to get a money objection. And you're going to get a timebased objection. You can almost guarantee those three are going to come up a large percentage of the time. So, we go over every one of those in the commitment questions before we even pitch them."   [1:01:18]  "Awesome. So, it sounds like you're pretty serious on doing this. Like obviously I know like I haven't earned your trust yet. I'm about to go into this and give you a whole overview, but if this is exactly like what you're looking for, it sounds like this is something that you know you want to do."   [1:01:38]  "Well, I'd I'd have to uh you know, blah blah blah, you know, whatever they say. And you say, 'Yeah, well, let me ask you this. um like is this something that you would make the decision with on like solely by yourself or is this something that you would typically talk to your your spouse or your business partner about?"   [1:02:00]  "And you hit it straight on, right? Oh yeah, I would typically talk to your spouse. Okay, got it. But I know you were saying earlier like the way that you guys divide your household, like she's in charge of the household, you're in charge of the business."   [1:02:20]  "Like I guess I would just be curious to know like if she said like don't do that, is that even a realistic outcome? Like would she ever tell you not to do something? No, I don't think she'd ever tell me not to do something. Okay, but let's just pretend she did."   [1:02:40]  "like she said no. Like I don't want to assume, but I would think your position on that would probably be like, 'Okay, honey. Well, I'm in charge of the business because I have the pressure of the finances.' You would probably explain to her why this is something you know you need to do"   [1:03:00]  "cuz like you were saying, this is a problem you wanted to solve for years and you don't have another solution to solve it, right? Yeah, it is. Okay. So, just to be clear, like it doesn't really matter if she was on board or not because the conviction that you have in doing this. Yeah."   [1:03:22]  "And and just to be super clear, like it sounds like this isn't even really something you need her permission for. It was just your immediate reaction when I asked if you talked to your wife about this type of stuff. Yeah, I think that's right."   [1:03:40]  "All right, cool. So now when you do that, you go into the pitch. Dude, you think they're going to have the nerve to be like, I got to talk to my wife after all of that. That's the commitment questions. You're gauging their commitment to this, the readiness to buy, right?"   [1:04:02]  "Hey, let me ask you this. I know you said you want to do this, but like can I be really blunt with you?" "You,"   [1:04:12]  "Dude, a lot of people tell me they want to do this and a lot of people don't. So, I guess one thing that I want to know because this is really important for the type of people that we work with because dude, this isn't like uh Barry Bond's baseball bat. Like, we can't just give it to you and you automatically hit home runs. You still have to learn how to hit, right?"   [1:04:38]  "So, we're looking for people that are willing to put in the work. I guess my question would be I know you want to do this but like on a level of priorities like how high of a priority is this for you" "business?"   [1:04:55]  "Oh, it's your top priority. So like just to be clear I I don't want to make any assumptions. Top priority to me means like there's nothing else more important than this one thing." "Yes."   [1:05:12]  "So, hypothetically, if there was like something else that was taking some of your time that prevented you from doing this, it sounds like what you're saying is you'd probably put that on hold before you would ever, you know, not do this." "Yeah, I think so. That's"   [1:05:32]  "Yeah. Yeah. No, that only makes sense to me based on everything you shared with me earlier about how it's costing you so much money a month. So, I just like to ask that to make sure that we're working with the right people. Cool. You move on."   [1:05:50]  "Now, you think that after you pitch, he's going to be like, 'Well, I don't have the time for this cuz I have this other thing.' Right? So, that's the purpose of the commitment questions and your object you're objection blocking before."   [1:06:08]  "Okay. Now, like I was saying earlier, yeah, Omar,"   [1:06:15]  "okay, so like I get this as well. So, when you you want to pre-own objection, they say, 'Well, yeah, man. It depends on the investment amount. So, I don't know what the price is, so it depends on how you tell.'"   [1:06:32]  "No, 100%, dude. And you're only logical for saying that. No one would ever commit to something without knowing how much it cost. But like cost aside, it sounds like you're saying to me that if the cost made sense to you, this is the perfect program," "right?"   [1:06:52]  "So that's the thing with my wife. It depends if it's up to a certain budget. So if it was above that budget, I need to discuss it with her. If it's below a certain budget, I would be able to make that call for myself."   [1:07:10]  "Yeah, dude. And by the way, like I really respect that cuz I'm engaged and I have a fiance and if I was going out there and blowing insane amounts of money without her knowing about it, that'd probably be an uncomfortable conversation. We're trying to help your family, not create more disagreements and have you sleeping on the couch, right?"   [1:07:35]  "So, that totally makes sense. I guess it really depends on like what would be considered an insane amount of money. What's the actual amount of money that you guys have discussed that you have to have a conversation over?"   [1:07:52]  "So, anything like it [clears throat] depends, but like anything above I'd said like two grand um would be something where I would have"   [1:08:05]  "You're broke. Click. No, I'm kidding. Yeah, they don't usually say that, dude. But like you you you [laughter] hear the you hear the point, though, right? Like so the"   [1:08:22]  "people people have said that to me I'll be like I'll literally be like hey man like hypothetically this is the right choice for you. How would you actually want to proceed?"   [1:08:35]  "So I would just I would say this. So if they really start pushing I say hey listen Omar like I told you at the beginning of this call that's again go back to the agenda frame. This is really important to us. Like I take what I do seriously and Omar I don't know you but I know you because there's only so many different conversations. I have about a dozen of them a day. I've been doing this for a long time."   [1:09:02]  "So, I feel like I understand you. I already committed to you. I'm going to make sure you have every piece of information to make an informed decision. I promise you, I'm not going to ask you to give me a blank check, right? We're going to cover all the pricing and details, but I guess I would say this, and I'm almost I'm going to get there in just a second, but I guess I would say this."   [1:09:28]  "Sounds like you're really price sensitive. Have you had a situation in the past where like you paid for something and didn't get what you were looking for? I'm just curious to ask because it's not very common and I have a lot of calls that people are like that worried about the actual price"   [1:09:50]  "because of course we're going to cover it before we hang up because the thing is dude everybody's a product of their own experiences and everybody has their own frame of reference and typically when someone's pushing really hard on price that's the reason right that's the reason"   [1:10:10]  "now at the end of the day I what you could see like dude it's muscle reflex for me I've been on so many sales calls I wouldn't even be able to describe how many I've been on. But the thing is, like I could just do this like the back of my hand with sales reps."   [1:10:30]  "Um, what you never want them to do is just like actually hold out. So, if you start really getting pressed, I would say what I just said. I'd be like, 'And by the way, dude, I'm not withholding information.' Like, I'm I'm just about to explain it, but like if if it's like really important to you to not go on past this call without knowing"   [1:10:55]  "it's a $8,000 program, but you know, it we we break that up in a few different ways. So, it kind of depends, which is usually why we don't talk about the the pricing until I kind of show you how it works, right?"   [1:11:15]  "Another way that you could do it, the best that if you have a single offer, you just have to lead with that and then just handle it really quickly and then go back to the same tie down. If he's like, absolutely, that won't work. Uh, yeah, no worries at"   [1:11:32]  "And and just to be clear, it's because that the $8,000 just doesn't physically exist in your bank account, or you're just like, there's no way possible that the $8,000 would actually have an ROI to get you the results that we're talking about. Because there's only really one of two things. Either there's not that amount of money in your bank account or you don't believe half the stuff that I'm saying, right?"   [1:12:00]  "And so you say that and you say, 'Okay, so let's do this. Dude, I'm always aware and fully accept the option of us not working together.' I think I actually said that in the beginning. So I'm completely indifferent. I want to help you, but I want to help the right people."   [1:12:22]  "If we get to the end here and you're like, 'Dude, this is just not going to work for me.' We could just part ways, but like I think what I would like to do instead is like, let's unpack this and see if you have a high confidence level that that will actually get you XYZ."   [1:12:42]  "Because like I can't tell anyone what they're going to make in their business, but I would ask you, it sound like you said like this is like one of the number one things that you have to solve in your business. I just can't imagine that that's only worth $8,000 if it's the number one priority. Right."   [1:13:05]  "So, you just start circling around and then you go right back to the offer." "Helpful." "Yeah."   [1:13:15]  "Deon, when you when you're asking that priority question, you said something along lines of where does this fall in terms of priority. Yeah."   [1:13:28]  "When you're asking that, you're asking where does solving this specific problem fall in terms of priority? Not like where does getting on podcast fall in terms of priority. Correct. So it's more about"   [1:13:45]  "So I don't listen I don't want to get hung up on like the specific of the questions because I'm just more trying to demonstrate the point of the commitment questions. But like dude depending on what it is. I'm trying to figure out the priority level of doing this thing."   [1:14:05]  "But you know what what do they say that that one marketer is like I forgot what they said. Like nobody's actually buying an 8 in drill. They're trying to buy an eight inch hole. You know what? You ever heard of that? Like they don't care about the fuck they do, bro. Nobody cares about podcasts."   [1:14:28]  "If you didn't know that they just care about the exposure and the only reason they care about the exposure, well, sometimes it's ego, but a lot of the times it's because they just want to make more money, right?"   [1:14:42]  "So, the point is when you're using that language, you're remind you're you're thinking of their core desires. You're thinking of their desired state. And you would use that in the priority question."   [1:15:00]  "So, let me So, let me ask you this, Devin. Like, I know podcast is a way to do this, but like it sounds like you're saying to me that you've identified since you're already doing a lot of paid media, the most important thing for you is to really establish your credibility and your authority"   [1:15:22]  "because you've determined that that's like the number one thing that's going to make a big difference in your business and you've never done that before. With all the different things that you could do to, you know, to to generate more money, I guess I would ask you like, how high of a priority is this for you? Right? I would just say it like that."   [1:15:48]  "it's tying back to their core desires. And guys, it's not that hard. It's not Jedi mind tricks. Like, they're going to tell you if it's a high priority or not, you know? I think that's uh you know, can I tell you guys something funny and we'll go right back to it."   [1:16:08]  "So, it's so funny, man, when I've on sales calls for engagements because people always think I'm like a fuck hypnotist or something. Like, I had so many times a potential client would be like, 'Yeah, I see what you're doing.' And I'm like, 'What the [laughter] fuck?' Brandon's laughing in the corner."   [1:16:32]  "It's so funny. They think I'm going to like trick them into doing business with me. I'm like, 'No, dude. I'm just like asking you a question.' Because it's that that's my point. Like it's not like this sneaky like you didn't say that."   [1:16:52]  "You know what Grant Cardone used to say, and the concept is true. He used to say, 'No single word you ever say will make or break a deal.' Right? And that's what he means. Like people think so specifically like, 'Should I say this one word? It doesn't really matter. Just ask the question, get the answer, and know what to do with it, you know."   [1:17:18]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Discovery Process Goal"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Expand the Gap & Amplify Desire   [1:17:22]  "All right, we'll move on. All right, so the discovery process goal. The goal of a discovery is to expand the gap and amplify desire. Okay, let me go to here and say one thing because we went on a little bit off track and I want to remember I said to pay attention to these next couple slides."   [1:17:48]  "I want you to remember this. Gap awareness. Expand the gap. Solution awareness. Amplify desire. Consequence and impact. You're creating the urgency. Commitment questions. You're objection blocking. The entire goal of a discovery process is expand the gap and amplify desire. Okay."   [1:18:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "How To Expand Gap & Amplify Desire"   NÚMEROS:  2 métodos   VISUAL:  1. Ask questions for self-realization, 2. Educate & Demonstrate Expertise   [1:18:20]  "[cough] [clears throat] I'm going to break this down. How do we do this? Well, we ask really good questions for them to realize it themselves. Right? If you say it, it might be true. If they say it, it's gospel. Right? You got to ask questions so they say it."   [1:18:42]  "Number two, the way you expand the gap is you educate to increase problem awareness. The way you amplify desire is you demonstrate expertise and build authority to create solution awareness. Right? Specifically though, building confidence in your solution."   [1:19:05]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Selfish Discovery = Deal Killer"   NÚMEROS:  20 minutos de perguntas   VISUAL:  Problema: sem demonstração de expertise, sem value sharing, apenas perguntas   [1:19:10]  "I want to show you something here really quick. This slide is like this is really important and again like there's no other way for me to emphasize the importance of this stuff other than saying like out of all the sales calls we listen to we identify this was one of the most important things."   [1:19:32]  "When you don't do that [clears throat] when you don't expand the gap and amplify desire we call it a selfish discovery. Now a selfish discovery means you didn't demonstrate expertise. You didn't create any light bulb moments and you didn't share any value at all. you just asked a bunch of questions for like 20 minutes straight. They didn't learn anything."   [1:20:00]  "Not only is it ineffective, it triggers sales resistance, but because prospects start thinking, 'Dude, get to the point. This is such a waste of time.' I've had people tell me that before, like, 'Well, dude, people don't let me ask my discovery questions because they want me to get to the offer.' I'm like, 'Dude, I don't even need to hear the call. It means it sucks.' Right?"   [1:20:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Quality of Answers = Quality of Questions"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Proporcionalidade direta entre qualidade de perguntas e respostas   [1:20:32]  "So, check this out." "[clears throat and cough]"   [1:20:38]  "The impact of your discovery and the quality of the answers will always be directly proportionate to the quality of the questions and how we ask them. Right?"   [1:20:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Example Call - High-End Mastermind"   NÚMEROS:  $30K-40K offer   VISUAL:  Case study de call de CEO para demonstrar expertise   [1:20:58]  "I want to skip ahead really quickly. All right? And then I'll go backwards because this slide's out of order. So I did, like I said, every once in a while I'll get back in the trenches and I'll take some calls because I want to show them."   [1:21:18]  "And we had a high-end mastermind offer. It was 30k, 30k, 40k, something like that. And uh and it was complex and it was a sophisticated avatar and so people were struggling with this. So, I got on the phone and I did a few example calls and I played these example calls to the sales team"   [1:21:42]  "and I said, you know, and then and guys, to be able to lead, by the way, in a sales organization by the CEO getting on the sales calls and just blowing it out of the water, like, dude, the conviction, the energy you instill in your team, they're just like, 'Let's fuck go.' You know, they're they're stoked."   [1:22:05]  "And I played this call and at the end of the discovery, I was playing it with no interruptions. I paused it, said, 'All right, did I pitch yet?' They're like, 'No.' I was like, 'Let me ask you something. What do you guys think right now would happen if I just asked them to buy?' They're like, 'Well, like without a pitch.' I'm like, 'Yeah.' I was just as like, 'So, you want to do this?'"   [1:22:32]  "There's kind of a pause and they're like, 'Dude, they probably would have said yes.' You know, I was like, 'Exactly.' And that's the point. The reason that is is because in the entire discovery, I'm like really showing them how much I know."   [1:22:52]  "And so it elevates their confidence level and they're like they're already thinking like fuck dude this is exactly what I was looking for. They're just like can't wait to get to the pitch and then you're showing them how much they don't know and in their head they're like dude this makes 100% sense why it's never worked in the past. This is what I was missing the entire time. They start getting stoked on it. Right?"   [1:23:20]  "That's what you have to create in a discovery process. And this is what's so often missed because these reps are just like, 'So, what are your goals?' Got it. Got it. So, why is that important to you? You know, people laugh because it's what we hear. Oh, got it. Got it. So, like, when's the first time you started thinking about this?"   [1:23:45]  "And dude, people are just so bugged by it. The amount of people that are triggered by shitty sales reps today is at like an all-time high. Make them feel like they learned something, dude."   [1:24:00]  "Like, okay, real thing. And I'm not saying Josh is so great, but this is the purpose of the topic. In some of my account uh acquisition like engagement calls, I have literally told people I have literally had people tell me they felt like they should pay me for consulting for the hour and it was a sales call, you know, and it's a little different in B2B. I'll give you that."   [1:24:28]  "But my point is it's just exuding authority and expertise where they're they leave the call like, 'Dude, I got to hire that guy.' Like that's it. Like I guess I guess that's the answer. I have to do it. Yeah." "Yeah."   [1:24:48]  "So I'm able to do that like with my discovery calls because I'm also consulting them on the back ends uh for the mastermind or for the offer. But my average sales guy does not have"   [1:25:02]  "product knowledge training." "Okay."   [1:25:08]  "Everyone says that it's product knowledge and training. And remember I said brand dynamics. Brand dynamics is a type of training we do which is sales training specific to the product knowledge on that offer."   [1:25:25]  "So you would you would brainstorm and train around concepts like well what happens if they had these problems we have a problem matrix we have a solution matrix which things make sense based on these pain points. So you're training around those types of things"   [1:25:45]  "and then the other really really important thing another really big mistake your sales reps are not positioning themselves as the expert. It's just such an amateur mistake that I see that really, if you could hear the tone in my voice that changed, really pisses me off"   [1:26:05]  "because let's say you're selling like on how to buy a business, they'll be like, 'Yeah, you know, so like you know, when I first got into this, I was I was like, 'Bro, you don't buy a business. You're on a sales call. We can see in your background. It looks really shitty. Like nobody's fooled that you're out here buying multi-million dollar businesses, right? That's not what you're supposed to do."   [1:26:32]  "You're supposed to You're a program advisor. Hey, I'm like the sales rep should be like, I'm not an expert on buying a business. I'm an expert on the program that works with people and teaches them how to buy a business. And then you elevate the subject subject matter expert in the fulfillment team, right?"   [1:26:55]  "I'm very confident that I'm going to be able to answer any question that you might have. But when it comes to actually doing this, dude, you'd laugh at me. Like, that's what our team's for. And you anchor all the value into the thing they're buying."   [1:27:15]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "What Happens BETWEEN Questions"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Foco negligenciado: o que fazer entre as perguntas   [1:27:20]  "All right, so we talked about questions.
