All right, now keep it up if you've heard them talk about what to do in between questions."   [1:27:42]  "Everyone's kind of confused. Julian, what did they tell you about in between questions?"   [1:27:50]  "They don't even know how to articulate. They're just like, I don't I'm stuck. They don't they don't they they're not able to even formulate a question of what to do. They just get stuck in the"   [1:28:05]  "Okay, so you haven't heard someone train on it. You've heard a rep complain about not knowing how to do it. Anyways, the point is this. Maybe you did, maybe you didn't. My point is this, not saying that this doesn't exist anywhere else in the world."   [1:28:22]  "All I'm saying is people don't focus on what to do in between the questions. They put so much emphasis on the question itself. And remember I was saying the got it, got it thing, right? And it's like an interrogation."   [1:28:38]  "So we actually train about what happens in between the questions and how you ingest the information, right? I'm only going to go a few minutes longer, guys."   [1:28:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "In Between Questions - Active Engagement"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Framework de como processar informação entre perguntas   [1:28:58]  "All right, in between the questions. So, top performers with high sales IQs exhibit a remarkable a remarkable ability to not just passively absorb information, but actively engage with it."   [1:29:18]  "They adeptly listen to responses, discern key insights, and skillfully reshape and refine the information in real time to make it more relevant and impactful within the conversation."   [1:29:35]  "Instead of simply acknowledging the information with the got it, they leverage it to deepen the understanding and to drive the sales process forward, demonstrating a mastery of both active listening and conversational refinement techniques."   [1:29:55]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "What NOT To Do Between Questions"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Lista de erros: Got it, Gotcha, Yep, interrogação, pitching prematuro   [1:30:00]  "So, here's what you don't do. Got it. Gotcha. Yep. Yep. Okay. Got it. Yep. It's not supposed to be an ear interrogation, right? An interrogation. Uh you don't want to just check boxes and read questions mindlessly."   [1:30:20]  "You're also not pitching in the discovery. You're not going to say in between the question, 'Yeah, perfect.' And so like our program, it has this. You're not pitching either."   [1:30:35]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "What TO DO Between Questions"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Lista de ações corretas: unpack, gain clarity, expand gap, amplify desire, frame   [1:30:40]  "What you are doing, you're unpacking the answers. You're gaining additional clarity. Hey, that made a lot of sense. Now, when you said that this was like really expensive, what exactly did you mean by that? Oh, okay. I'm going to show you an example in a second."   [1:31:00]  "You're unpacking it. You're expanding the gap. You're amplifying desire. You're framing in and out of the questions. You're actively engaging in the information. and you're organically flowing between the questions. Most the time, if you're really good in sales, they shouldn't know when you're going to ask the next question. You're kind of just weaving through the conversation and then you arrive at the next one."   [1:31:30]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Example: Framing In & Out of Questions"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Exemplo completo de como fazer framing entre perguntas   [1:31:35]  "All right, skipping through some of this. So, all right. So, here's an example, I think. Um, speaking of All right, so here's an example of what I was just saying. So in between the questions, so you'll see the root question is at the end."   [1:31:55]  "Here's the question. What can you tell me about your current management motion or how you take extreme ownership of that? But if I were to say it in a conversation because the in between the questions and I'm unpacking it, I'd say something like, 'Yeah, I mean that makes total sense.'"   [1:32:18]  "Listen, in our experience, so many clients, they put like way too much emphasis on the sales reps themselves, right? And the thing with sales reps is the top performing sales rep can go from being at the top of the leaderboard to having their pedagoguana die over the weekend and now they're just fuck the bed for weeks. It's like chicken soup to chicken poop, right?"   [1:32:45]  "So, I guess my point is the real answer isn't about having great sales reps. It's about having a badass sales management team that can constantly evaluate, manage, and develop the rest of the underperforming reps."   [1:33:02]  "So, it's not just about like hiring sales reps. I guess my question is what can you tell me about your current sales management motion or how you've taken extreme ownership of that? Right? That's what I mean when I say in between the questions framing in and out."   [1:33:22]  "I'm also I skip these because I'm trying to go faster to in but there's also a lot of question techniques. These are authoritative questions. These are driving and leading questions. I'm basically controlling the response. I almost always know what I'm going to what they're going to say based on the way that I ask the question, right?"   [1:33:48]  "But anyways, the point is how much more powerful is that? That's what it means to expand the gap. I didn't say how how do you manage your sales reps and and then they might say some delusional fuck like really well, you know"   [1:34:05]  "but if I train them on this and I'm I'm I'm I'm like educating them on how it works, they're like, damn, he understands my pain points. We were talking about me, Eric, Brandon, we were talking in the hallway about like a sales rep who's great will like move or their like girlfriend will break up with them or something and their close rate will go down."   [1:34:32]  "And you have to articulate that so they know how well you understand it. And then you frame it with the question, right?"   [1:34:42]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Fluid Conversations - Another Example"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Exemplo de objection blocking através de framing   [1:34:48]  "Fluid conversations. I'll give you one more. Let's say that I'm trying to objection block with uh what does this one say? You can't go in on one rep. All right. So, let's say that I'm trying to objection block from people that are like, I'll just hire a recruiting company."   [1:35:10]  "So, I'll say something like this. Yeah. I mean, speaking of sales rep, obviously talent acquisition is extremely important, right? Like you have to start with great sales reps. It's a core competency of ours and a sales partner has to have that."   [1:35:30]  "It's crazy though how many sales agencies just hire through like a warm network cir networking circle and they don't have a predictable way to find talent. So we treat talent acquisition better than most companies treat customer acquisition, right?"   [1:35:50]  "Deep candidate generation, multiple hiring funnels, lots of friction, only finding the top candidates. Because the truth about why the traditional recruiting model doesn't work for sales, this is true, is because you don't find the best talent, you develop the best talent. It's all about your rep development and training motion. Right now, sales recruiting, guys, it's like media buying."   [1:36:18]  "You can't go all in on one rep. You have to test multiple reps and you scale the top performers. So, if you don't have a way to consistently hire multiple reps, you're not going to find the top performers."   [1:36:35]  "So, all that said, what have you experienced in terms of recruiting reps in the past, right? They are going to basically tell me what I just said because it's on it's on their mind. They understand that I know it. They're going to be like, 'Yeah, that makes sense.' They're connecting the dots. This is what we're doing in our discovery process."   [1:37:00]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Transition & Follow-Up Best Practices"   NÚMEROS:  24-48 horas window   VISUAL:  Framework de transição para pitch e setup de follow-up   [1:37:05]  "All right, let me get to the end here, guys. I'm just going to skip through it. We do a transition into the pitch. Um, I already read pitch personalization, so I'm just going to end with this one thing, one slide."   [1:37:22]  "People always want to know when do you know to push for a follow-up or push for a close? All right, best practices for setting a follow-up. So, when you're or sorry, I'll just talk about first of all, setting the follow-up."   [1:37:42]  "So, when you're setting a follow-up call, you like if you just did the full discovery and you're setting up the follow-up to do the pitch, you still want to give them a sizzle pitch. like give them something. Give them an overview. Otherwise, you're not creating enough interest for them to show up to the next call. Okay?"   [1:38:05]  "If you have a discovery call and then a closing call, don't just finish your questions and then set up a follow-up. They're going to be like, 'What the fuck dude? I was just on here for 30 minutes. You didn't tell me anything.'" "Sometimes they'll ghost you for resentment."   [1:38:25]  "So, you got to give them a little pitch. Then you want to do a strong trial close where you're basically saying like, 'Yeah, it sounds like to me it, but you know, correct me if I'm wrong. On that next call, if this checks all your boxes, this is something that's sounding like it's right up your alley.' Right? So, you're getting that commitment towards a follow-up. You're setting strong expectations without sales resistance."   [1:38:52]  "Um, I said this earlier, guys. So, you should set the follow-up within 24 to 48 hours. I know I already said that. Treat it like an objection. Go back now. Now, it makes more sense. by the way, cuz I showed you all the commitment questions."   [1:39:12]  "If they push back on this, you're like, 'Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa. You said this is your number one priority.' Like, you're not free for a week, you know? And you could have that conversation with them."   [1:39:28]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Content Between Calls Strategy"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Propósito de enviar conteúdo: awareness + show rate + reason for call   [1:39:32]  "Content in between calls, right? Um, now, so the content in between calls, by the way, is this is why it's so important. I know it's obvious. Even a lot of people do it that don't know why they do it. Believe it or not, it's not just to increase the level of awareness."   [1:39:52]  "One of the reasons you do it is because it really increases the show rate to your follow-up call because it gives a reason you needed the follow-up call. Say, 'Hey, so here's what we do from here.' You explain and get a bunch of buy in on the content."   [1:40:12]  "Say, typically like what we find is like it makes so much more sense to have this conversation actually after you understand the strategy that we teach just so you can make sure that this is right for you and this is what you want. So you frame it as a benefit for them"   [1:40:32]  "and now it's not weird because if you don't if you don't set something up like this in their head they're like well there's nothing else to talk about. You're just going to like close me on the next call, right? And then they they get sales resistance. Yeah. One sec."   [1:40:52]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Content Rules: Don't Give Everything"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Aviso: não dar inclusions completas e preço no conteúdo   [1:40:58]  "So I'll just finish this. So you get you get the commitment to the content. Do not give all the inclusions and the price in the content. Big mistake. You'll have a high ghost rate. There needs to be a reason they need you to get on the next call."   [1:41:18]  "Otherwise, a lot of the times they're like, 'All right, cool. Well, good to know. I'll reach out if I need this, you know, and they just they they would ghost you. So, uh, so that's that."   [1:41:32]  📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ─────────────────────────────  TEXTO:  "Follow-Up Call Structure"   NÚMEROS:  Não especificados   VISUAL:  Information Confirmation + Recommit + Pitch + Close   [1:41:38]  "Um, and then when you get on the follow-up call, you start with the follow-up, the information confirmation. Um, you recommit them with the commitment questions again. So, you're basically anchoring back. Hey, remember on the last call, let me just give a quick recap."   [1:42:00]  "On the last call, we talked about XX and X. You like re help them relive that pain. You bring it up again. You remind them of what they said. you told them how uh how much of a priority they told you it was. You rebrup all of that, tie them down, get them to admit it again, and then you go into your pitch."   [1:42:25]  "If you skip all of that and go into your pitch, your close rate will be atrocious. Yeah. Take all the value from the discovery call, bring it right back to that, then you do the pitch, then go to the closing sequence."   [1:42:45]  "All right, guys. We're I'm going to end it right here just because there's like there's a little bit more and it's too long and it's already late. So, uh, yeah. I mean, Jeremy, I'll say the same thing, dude. I'll stand up here and do Q&A, but like it's time for everyone else to go to dinner, I think." "That's right."   [1:43:05]  "Here we go." "Okay. Give Josh a round of applause." "[applause]"     📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO   NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS: $3M+/mês em revenue do maior cliente $400-500K/mês em offer de industrial development $500K liquid de leads qualificados $50 cost per call (industrial development) $30K-40K high-end mastermind offer 3 anos para ROI (exemplo de CPR ruim) $8K exemplo de preço de programa $2K threshold de decisão conjugal (exemplo) 60 minutos one-call close vs 30 minutos two-call 24-48 horas window para follow-up 20 minutos de discovery "selfish" 30-60 segundos sizzle pitch 3-4 pilares principais do offer 14 dias aging (exemplo do steak) 12 ounce (exemplo do steak)   ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:   Pitch Design Formula: Offer = o que você dá Pitch = como você vende Fórmula: Strategy + Psychology + Creativity Estrutura: Story - Pitch - Pillars Start with end in mind, reverse engineer   Story-Pitch-Pillars Framework: Story = Mirror Existence Narrative:  História onde prospect vê sua própria história refletida Pitch = Sizzle Pitch:  30-60 segundos, elevator pitch diferenciado Pillars:  3-4 componentes principais que encapsulam o offer   Pitch Design Questionnaire (3 Sets): Subject Matter Expert/Credibility/Background Domain Expertise/Subject Matter Focus Offer/Proprietary Method/USPs/Key Differentiators   Pitch Personalization (Step 7 of 10): Connect feature → benefit → core desire "Remember earlier when you said X? That's exactly why we built Y" Tie down: "Wouldn't that make a big difference?" Confirmation: "So your confidence level is way higher now?"   One vs Two Call Close Decision Factors: Level of Awareness:  Baixa awareness = two-call CPR (Clear Path to Revenue/Results):  Quanto mais claro, mais one-call Show Rate:  Show rate ruim = two-call (15min triage) Complexity:  Alta complexidade = two-call   Anatomy of Talk Track: Warm-up Agenda Frame Discovery Questions Information Confirmation (strategic transition) Transition to Follow-up/Close Pitch (Story-Pitch-Pillars) Closing Sequence   Agenda Frame Origins: Vem do "Pack Break" do timeshare Versão moderna remove sales resistance Set expectations upfront Control the process Hook interest para end of call Create opportunity awareness   Types of Questions & Their Significance:   Tipo Significância Probing Direct conversation Situational Find pain points & core desires (current vs desired state) Gap Awareness Expand the gap, twist the knife Solution Awareness Amplify desire for pitch Consequence/Impact Create urgency & certainty Commitment Objection blocking, gauge readiness   3 Main Objections (Pre-blocked in Commitment Questions): Spousal/Business Partner Money/Budget Time/Capacity   Objection Blocking Examples: Spousal:  "Would she really tell you not to do something? If she did, wouldn't you explain why you need this?" Priority:  "Top priority means nothing else is more important. Would you put other things on hold?" Money:  "Cost aside, if the cost made sense, is this the perfect program?"   Discovery Process Goal: Expand the Gap  (gap awareness questions) Amplify Desire  (solution awareness questions)   How To Expand Gap & Amplify Desire: Ask questions for self-realization ("If they say it, it's gospel") Educate to increase problem awareness Demonstrate expertise to create solution awareness Build authority and confidence in solution   Selfish Discovery (Deal Killer): No expertise demonstration No light bulb moments No value sharing Just 20 minutes of questions Triggers sales resistance "Got it. Got it. Got it." interrogation style   What To Do BETWEEN Questions: Unpack answers Gain additional clarity Expand the gap Amplify desire Frame in and out Actively engage with information Organic flow (they shouldn't know when next question comes)   Question Techniques: Authoritative questions Driving and leading questions Control the response Know what they'll say based on how you ask   Follow-Up Best Practices: Give sizzle pitch before setting follow-up (don't just end with questions) Strong trial close for commitment Set within 24-48 hours (treat pushback like objection) Send content between calls DON'T give all inclusions + price in content  (high ghost rate) Need reason for them to get on next call   Follow-Up Call Structure: Information Confirmation (recap last call) Recommit with commitment questions (anchor back to pain) Pitch Closing Sequence   Rep Positioning: WRONG:  "When I first got into buying businesses..." RIGHT:  "I'm not an expert on buying businesses. I'm an expert on the PROGRAM that teaches how to buy businesses." Elevate subject matter expert/fulfillment team Anchor all value into the thing they're buying   ESTRUTURA:   Main Reasons People Lose Deals: Weak Discovery:  Not asking good questions, checking boxes, interrogation, no expertise demonstration, don't expand gap/amplify desire Weak Pitch:  Result of weak discovery, creating objections, pitching features non-compellingly, doesn't align with gap/desires, value perception issue Can't Close:  Don't ask for business, don't know how to enroll, awkward around money   The Big Three (Focus Areas): Strong Discovery Irresistible Personalized Pitch Closing Sequence   Generic Pitch vs Irresistible Pitch: Generic (Deal Killing):  "Let me share my screen... on demand curriculum... coaching calls... resource vault..." (could be ANY offer) Irresistible:  Differentiates, positions uniquely, creates FOMO, vivid mental imagery, shows WHY different   Steak Example Breakdown: Generic:  "12 ounce cut, cooked to liking, served with mashed potatoes and vegetables, choice of sauces" Irresistible:  "Signature steak for luxurious dining experience, hand selected prime cut, 14 days aged, proprietary seasoning, artisan flame technique, char locks flavors, won't find anywhere else, knife cuts like butter, unique mushroom blend, explosion of flavor"   Marketing vs Sales Disconnect: Marketing spends tons on USPs, positioning, key differentiators Sales ignores all of it Should be congruent:  Through line from ads → content → sales call Creates confirmation bias, reinforcement bias Use marketing USPs to develop sales pitch   CPR (Clear Path to Revenue) Examples: Bad CPR:  3 years to make money (industrial development) - creates massive objections Good CPR:  Quick wins, months not years, clear milestones Fix:  Break into phases, focus on first exit (raw land to contractor)   Content Between Calls Purpose: Increase awareness Increase show rate  (gives reason for next call) Frame as benefit for them Remove sales resistance about "just closing me next call"   Influence-Based Leadership Application: Sales principles = Leadership principles Guide to solution, don't tell Listen before being confident in solution Seek to understand Get people more of what they want Objection handling: listen and diffuse, don't argue   "If You Say It, It Might Be True. If They Say It, It's Gospel."   Authority & Expertise Demonstration: Make them feel like they learned something Create light bulb moments Show how much you know Elevate their confidence in you "Felt like they should pay for consulting on sales call"   High-End Mastermind Example ($30K-40K): CEO gets in trenches to show team Plays call with no pitch "What if I just asked them to buy right now?" Team: "They probably would have said yes" Point:  Discovery so good they're already sold   Chicken Soup to Chicken Poop: Top performer's pet dies = worst performer next week Use this in framing to show you understand their pain "Sales rep can go from top of leaderboard to fucking the bed for weeks"   DADOS FINANCEIROS: Largest client: $3M+/month revenue when brought in Typical clients: couple hundred K/month Industrial development: $400-500K/month (not great, walked away) Cost per call examples: $50 (industrial development) Lead quality: $500K liquid for $50/call (crazy good) High-end mastermind: $30K-40K offer Example program price: $8K Budget threshold example: $2K for spousal discussion Timeshare purchases: $50K typical ROI timeframe bad example: 3 years Call duration: 60min one-call vs 30min two-call   Psychology & Concepts: Mirror Narrative:  Their story reflected in yours Confirmation Bias:  Congruent messaging creates validation Reinforcement Bias:  Consistent through line reinforces belief FOMO:  Psychological fear of missing out Pack Break:  Breaking the pact to not buy (timeshare origin) Sales Resistance:  Created by poor agenda setting or selfish discovery Opportunity Awareness:  Hook interest early, deliver at end Problem Awareness:  Educate to expand gap Solution Awareness:  Demonstrate expertise to amplify desire Urgency Creation:  Consequence questions Certainty Creation:  Consequence questions Active Listening:  Not passive absorption Conversational Refinement:  Reshape information in real-time Trial Close:  Get commitment before follow-up Anchor Back:  Recommit to pain points on follow-up   Key Phrases & Scripts: "Remember earlier when you said X? That's exactly why we built Y." "Wouldn't that make a big difference?" "How does your confidence level change knowing X?" "So just to confirm, your confidence level is way higher now?" "If they say it, it's gospel." "A confused mind doesn't buy." "No single word you say will make or break a deal." "You're broke. Click." (joke when they say low budget) "I don't want to be right. I want to be rich."   DURAÇÃO:  1:08:10  FORMATO:  Apresentação presencial em mastermind (Miami) - Part 2 sobre Sales Process
